results.title: MEASUREMENT AND CONTROL METHODS IN ELECTRICAL ENGINEERING
results.citation: Taisiia Serdenko et al. (2025) MEASUREMENT AND CONTROL METHODS IN ELECTRICAL ENGINEERING doi:10.23939/istcmtm2025.02.012
results.venue: Measuring Equipment and Metrology
results.abstract: The article focuses on innovative measurement and control methods in electrical power engineering, specifically addressing challenges of power quality, signal diagnostics, and automation within smart grids. Emphasis is placed on wavelet analysis, smart metering, IoT integration, and automated control systems. These technologies are examined in the context of enhancing the adaptability and efficiency of modern electrical systems in line with Industry 4.0 requirements. Particular emphasis is placed on wavelet analysis, which serves as a universal tool for diagnosing non-stationary electrical signals, assessing power quality, and detecting harmonic distortions. Thanks to its capability for time-frequency localization, wavelet analysis enables effective signal processing and facilitates tasks such as transient process monitoring, voltage flicker analysis, and improving the accuracy of electrical measurements. This methodology opens new prospects for maintaining the stability of energy systems even under the challenging conditions of renewable energy integration. Special attention is given to the analysis of the role of smart technologies in contemporary energy systems. The advantages of Smart Metering systems—which ensure the automatic collection, analysis, and real-time transmission of energy consumption data—are discussed. This enables efficient management of energy resource distribution, reduces energy losses, and enhances transparency in the relationships between consumers and suppliers. The integration of Smart Metering with Internet of Things (IoT) technologies contributes to the creation of adaptive systems capable of responding to changing conditions in real time, thereby ensuring the stability and efficiency of smart grids. The article also explores the prospects of automated control systems that incorporate intelligent data collection devices and adaptive control algorithms. These systems significantly improve monitoring and diagnostics, facilitate the integration of renewable energy sources, and enhance power quality indicators. In particular, the automation of control processes and the implementation of machine learning technologies open new opportunities for forecasting the behavior of energy systems and increasing their resilience. The solutions presented in the study are aimed at creating adaptive, resilient, and high-tech energy systems that meet the modern challenges of Industry 4.0. Through the integration of wavelet analysis, Smart Metering, IoT, and automated control systems, effective management of energy resources, network stability, and the optimization of energy resource usage in the global energy system can be achieved.

results.title: SMART GIC - RESILIENT POWER GRIDS THROUGH GEOPHYSICAL SENSING AND GRAPH-BASED MODELING
results.citation: CHRISTIAN DAVISON DIRISU et al. (2025) SMART GIC - RESILIENT POWER GRIDS THROUGH GEOPHYSICAL SENSING AND GRAPH-BASED MODELING doi:10.70382/tijert.v09i5.010
results.venue: International Journal of Engineering Research and Technology
results.abstract: Geomagnetically induced currents (GICs), which are ground-level quasi-direct currents induced by space-weather phenomena, pose a serious threat to high-voltage power transmission grids, leading to transformer saturation, reactive power loss, and cascading outages during geomagnetic storms. This paper presents a smart, graph-theory-based framework that bridges real-time geophysical sensing with topology-informed grid modeling to protect power networks proactively. We represent the power grid as a graph data structure: substations and transformers are nodes, and transmission lines are edges; we then embed real-time geoelectric field measurements and space-weather forecasts. This feeds into a graph-based GIC algorithm motivated by sparsity principles of sparse-matrix computations and graph neural networks (GNNs), and trained against comparison grids, such as IEEE test systems and China's 500 kV Shandong network. Validation compared performance and accuracy using real storm instances in the UK and Poland. Our method, enhanced with GNN, detects 20% more high-risk grid sections than traditional approaches and enables optimized GIC mitigation hardware placement (e.g., series capacitors, DC-blockers), maintaining computation speed of approximately 18× owing to graph-parallelization. The models also estimate economic trade-offs, enabling cost-efficient investment in grid hardening and operating decisions in real-time during geomagnetic disturbances. This geophysics, electrical engineering, graph theory, and data science-backed cross-disciplinary approach provides an integrated, scalable solution for GIC threat prediction, mitigation, and management. Our findings verify its promise to enhance the resilience of the grid and ensure a secure electric supply in the age of rising space-weather uncertainty.

results.title: Voltage Control Partitioning Method Based on Weighted Fast-Newman for Ultra High Voltage Direct Current Power Grid
results.citation: Tianhua Chen et al. (2024) Voltage Control Partitioning Method Based on Weighted Fast-Newman for Ultra High Voltage Direct Current Power Grid doi:10.1109/IFEEA64237.2024.10878526
results.venue: 2024 11th International Forum on Electrical Engineering and Automation (IFEEA)
results.abstract: With the infeed UHV-DC merges into the power grid, the division of voltage control partitioning is more challenging, which is an important step in the secondary voltage control of power system. In this paper, the infeed UHV-DC node is equivalent to a reactive power supply, the weighted Fast-Newman algorithm is used to merge the reactive power sources through the maximum distance difference degree. The proposed method makes load nodes be divided with voltage reactive sensitivity and the average power factor. Which not only reflects the power grid by the center of hub AC bus control requirements, and also conforms to the principle of power grid control hierarchical control. By using the method the control partition result is more adaptable and the influence of UHV-DC transmission power of the partition is considered as well. The rationality of the proposed method is verified by the simulation results of IEEE39 node system.

papergraph_input.title: Voltage Control Partitioning Method Based on Weighted Fast-Newman for Ultra High Voltage Direct Current Power Grid
papergraph_input.citation: Tianhua Chen et al. (2024) Voltage Control Partitioning Method Based on Weighted Fast-Newman for Ultra High Voltage Direct Current Power Grid doi:10.1109/IFEEA64237.2024.10878526
papergraph_input.venue: 2024 11th International Forum on Electrical Engineering and Automation (IFEEA)
papergraph_input.abstract: With the infeed UHV-DC merges into the power grid, the division of voltage control partitioning is more challenging, which is an important step in the secondary voltage control of power system. In this paper, the infeed UHV-DC node is equivalent to a reactive power supply, the weighted Fast-Newman algorithm is used to merge the reactive power sources through the maximum distance difference degree. The proposed method makes load nodes be divided with voltage reactive sensitivity and the average power factor. Which not only reflects the power grid by the center of hub AC bus control requirements, and also conforms to the principle of power grid control hierarchical control. By using the method the control partition result is more adaptable and the influence of UHV-DC transmission power of the partition is considered as well. The rationality of the proposed method is verified by the simulation results of IEEE39 node system.

results.title: Modeling and Enhancement of Seismic Resilience for Ultra High Voltage Converter Station
results.citation: Zhihang Xue et al. (2024) Modeling and Enhancement of Seismic Resilience for Ultra High Voltage Converter Station doi:10.1109/ICPEE64457.2024.10949981
results.venue: 2024 8th International Conference on Power and Energy Engineering (ICPEE)
results.abstract: The power system is a critical infrastructure for the development of the national economy, and earthquakes pose a significant threat to the operational safety of the power grid. As a key node for long-distance electrical power transmission, the ability of ultra-high voltage (UHV) converter stations to withstand earthquake and recover swiftly afterward has garnered considerable attention within the industry. This article focuses on the modeling and enhancement methods for the seismic resistance of UHV converter station. Firstly, a component diagram of the UHV converter stations is established, and the post-earthquake transmission function index for individual station is defined. Subsequently, based on seismic intensity data, the failure probability of equipment within each branch at a specified seismic intensity is calculated. Additionally, the seismic resilience index R of the UHV converter station is defined, considering the post-earthquake recovery process. Finally, using data from a specific ±800 kV UHV converter station as a case study, its seismic resilience is assessed. Through comparative analysis, methods to enhance seismic resilience are explored. The research holds significant guidance for enhancing the seismic resilience of the UHV voltage converter stations.

results.title: Welding technology of ultra high voltage transmission steel pipe tower butt welded joint by MIG welding
results.citation: Hanyang et al. (2018) Welding technology of ultra high voltage transmission steel pipe tower butt welded joint by MIG welding doi:10.1088/1755-1315/199/5/052002
results.venue: IOP Conference Series: Earth and Environment
results.abstract: In twenty-first Century, China vigorously promoted the research and construction of AC and DC transmission technology in order to ensure the optimal allocation of energy resources in a large scale[1]. The transmission line is the core of the power grid, which plays the role of transmission, transformation and distribution of electric energy, and each tower is the core component of the transmission line. At present, because the force of the steel tube tower is more reasonable, it has been widely used in the construction of UHV, and the link of the key position of the steel tube tower is welded, so the quality of the welding is of great significance to the construction of the whole network frame. For the welding of the steel tube tower, the manual semi automatic CO2 gas shielded welding is mostly used. Because of the characteristics of the protective gas medium, it is easy to produce a large number of splashes and not easy to clean up during the welding process. It is easy to make the weld penetration rate and forming decline. It is effective to use the melting pole mixture gas protection welding, If we can make a scientific welding standard, it is possible to reduce the occurrence of welding spatter, achieve the purpose of improving welding efficiency and controlling manufacturing cost.

results.title: Review of Power Electronic Solutions for Dielectric Barrier Discharge Applications
results.citation: Hyeongmeen Baik et al. (2025) Review of Power Electronic Solutions for Dielectric Barrier Discharge Applications doi:10.1109/TPEL.2026.3658517
results.abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive review of dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) power supply topologies, aiming to bridge the gap between DBD applications and power electronics design. Two key aspects are examined: the dependence of the DBD electrical model on reactor geometry, and application-driven requirements for injected waveform characteristics, including shapes, voltage amplitude, frequency, and modulation techniques. On this basis, the paper systematically reviews two major categories of power supplies: sinusoidal types comprising transformerless and transformer-based resonant inverters, and pulsed power supplies (PPSs). The review summarizes performance trade-offs, highlights untested topologies and emerging applications, and offers guidance for advancing high-performance DBD power supply design for next-generation systems.

results.title: Electric Vehicle Attack Impact on Power Grid Operation
results.citation: Mohammad Ali Sayed et al. (2021) Electric Vehicle Attack Impact on Power Grid Operation doi:10.1016/j.ijepes.2021.107784
results.abstract: The increasing need for reducing greenhouse gas emissions and the drive for green cities have promoted the use of electric vehicles due to their environmental benefits. In fact, countries have set their own targets and are offering incentives for people to purchase EVs as opposed to traditional gasoline-powered cars. Manufacturers have been hastily deploying charging stations to meet the charging requirements of the EVs on the road. This rapid deployment has contributed to the EV ecosystem's lack of proper security measures, raising multiple questions related to the power grid security and vulnerability. In this paper, we offer a complete examination of the EV ecosystem from the vulnerability to the attacks and finally the solutions. We start by examining the existing vulnerabilities in the EV ecosystem that can be exploited to control the EV charging and launch attacks against the power grid. We then discuss the non-linear nature of the EV charging load and simulate multiple attacks that can be launched against the power grid using these EVs. EV loads have high reactive power demand which can have a larger impact on the grid compared to residential loads. We perform simulations on two power grids and demonstrate that while the grid can recover after a 48 MW attack utilizing traditional residential loads, a smaller 30 MW EV load attack can completely destabilize the system. Finally, we suggest several patches for the existing vulnerabilities and discuss two methods aimed at detecting EV attacks.

results.title: A Hybrid Transmission Grid Architecture Enabling Efficient Optimal Power Flow
results.citation: Matthias Hotz et al. (2016) A Hybrid Transmission Grid Architecture Enabling Efficient Optimal Power Flow doi:10.1109/TPWRS.2015.2507639
results.abstract: The recent rise of electricity generation based on renewable energy sources increases the demand for transmission capacity. Capacity expansion via the upgrade of transmission line capacity, e.g., by conversion to a high-voltage direct current (HVDC) line, is an attractive option. In this paper, it is shown that if the upgrade to HVDC is applied systematically to selected transmission lines across the grid, a hybrid architecture is obtained that enables an efficient and globally optimal solution of the optimal power flow (OPF) problem. More precisely, for conventional meshed AC transmission grids the OPF problem is nonconvex and in general NP-hard, rendering it hard to solve. We prove that after the upgrade to the proposed hybrid architecture, the same mesh topology facilitates an exact convex relaxation of the OPF problem, enabling its globally optimal solution with efficient polynomial time algorithms. This OPF method is then employed in simulations, which demonstrate that the hybrid architecture can increase the effective transmission capacity and substantially reduce the generation costs, even compared to the AC grid with optimal transmission switching.

results.title: Decomposition Problem in Process of Selective Identification and Localization of Voltage Fluctuations Sources in Power Grids
results.citation: Piotr Kuwałek (2022) Decomposition Problem in Process of Selective Identification and Localization of Voltage Fluctuations Sources in Power Grids doi:10.1109/ICHQP53011.2022.9808778
results.abstract: Voltage fluctuations are common disturbances in power grids, therefore the effective and selective process of identification and localization of individual voltage fluctuations sources is necessary for the minimization of such disturbances. Selectivity in the process of identification and localization disturbing loads is possible by the use cascade of blocks: demodulation, decomposition and propagation assessment. The effectiveness of this approach is closely related to the used method of decomposition. The paper presents the problem of decomposition process for the selected method of selective identification and localization of voltage fluctuation sources, in which the algorithm of enhanced empirical wavelet transform (EEWT) is used as the decomposition method. The paper presents selected research results from the real power grid, for which the result of selected approach causes mistakes in the process of identification and localization of voltage fluctuations sources. The potential causes of such mistakes related to the decomposition process are discussed on the basis of obtained research results.

results.title: Laboratory Setup for Testing Low-Frequency Disturbances of Power Quality
results.citation: Piotr Kuwałek et al. (2024) Laboratory Setup for Testing Low-Frequency Disturbances of Power Quality doi:10.1109/CPE-POWERENG60842.2024.10604360
results.abstract: Low-frequency disturbances of power quality are one of the most common disturbances in the power grid. These disturbances are most often the result of the impact of power electronic and energy-saving devices, the number of which is increasing significantly in the power grid. Due to the simultaneous operation of various types of loads in the power grid, various types of simultaneous disturbances of power quality occur, such as voltage fluctuations and distortions. Therefore, there is a need to analyze this type of simultaneous interaction. For this purpose, a special and complementary laboratory setup has been prepared, which allows for the examination of actual states occurring in modern power networks. Selected research results are presented for this laboratory setup, which determine its basic properties. Possible applications and possibilities of the laboratory setup are presented from the point of view of current challenges.

results.title: Parameterized Linear Power Flow for High Fidelity Voltage Solutions in Distribution Systems
results.citation: Marija Marković et al. (2022) Parameterized Linear Power Flow for High Fidelity Voltage Solutions in Distribution Systems doi:10.1109/TPWRS.2022.3218263
results.abstract: This paper introduces a new model for highly accurate distribution voltage solutions, coined as a parameterized linear power flow model. The proffered model is grounded on a physical model of linear power flow equations, and uses learning-aided parameterization to increase the fidelity of voltage solutions over a wide range of operating points. To this end, the closed-form analytic solution of the parameterization approach is obtained via a Gaussian Process using a deliberately small input sample and without the need for recomputation. The resulting "self-adjusting" parameter is system-specific and controls how accurate the proposed power flow equations are according to loading conditions. Under a certain value of the resulting parameter, the proposed model can fully recover the linearized formulation of a specialized branch flow model for radial distribution systems, the so-called simplified DistFlow model. Numerical examples are provided to illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed model as well as the improvement in solution accuracy for voltage magnitudes over the simplified DistFlow model and several other linear power flow models, at multiple loading levels. Simulations were carried out on six small- and medium-sized test systems.

results.title: Output Voltage Response Improvement and Ripple Reduction Control for Input-parallel Output-parallel High-Power DC Supply
results.citation: Jianhui Meng et al. (2023) Output Voltage Response Improvement and Ripple Reduction Control for Input-parallel Output-parallel High-Power DC Supply doi:10.1109/TPEL.2023.3290590
results.abstract: A three-phase isolated AC-DC-DC power supply is widely used in the industrial field due to its attractive features such as high-power density, modularity for easy expansion and electrical isolation. In high-power application scenarios, it can be realized by multiple AC-DC-DC modules with Input-Parallel Output-Parallel (IPOP) mode. However, it has the problems of slow output voltage response and large ripple in some special applications, such as electrophoresis and electroplating. This paper investigates an improved Adaptive Linear Active Disturbance Rejection Control (A-LADRC) with flexible adjustment capability of the bandwidth parameter value for the high-power DC supply to improve the output voltage response speed. To reduce the DC supply ripple, a control strategy is designed for a single module to adaptively adjust the duty cycle compensation according to the output feedback value. When multiple modules are connected in parallel, a Hierarchical Delay Current Sharing Control (HDCSC) strategy for centralized controllers is proposed to make the peaks and valleys of different modules offset each other. Finally, the proposed method is verified by designing a 42V/12000A high-power DC supply, and the results demonstrate that the proposed method is effective in improving the system output voltage response speed and reducing the voltage ripple, which has significant practical engineering application value.

results.title: Robust Power Flow and Three-Phase Power Flow Analyses
results.citation: Amritanshu Pandey et al. (2018) Robust Power Flow and Three-Phase Power Flow Analyses doi:10.1109/TPWRS.2018.2863042
results.abstract: Robust simulation is essential for reliable operation and planning of transmission and distribution power grids. At present, disparate methods exist for steady-state analysis of the transmission (power flow) and distribution power grid (three-phase power flow). Due to the non-linear nature of the problem, it is difficult for alternating current (AC) power flow and three-phase power flow analyses to ensure convergence to the correct physical solution, particularly from arbitrary initial conditions, or when evaluating a change (e.g. contingency) in the grid. In this paper, we describe our equivalent circuit formulation approach with current and voltage variables that models both the positive sequence network of the transmission grid and three-phase network of the distribution grid without loss of generality. The proposed circuit models and formalism enable the extension and application of circuit simulation techniques to solve for the steady-state solution with excellent robustness of convergence. Examples for positive sequence transmission and three-phase distribution systems, including actual 75k+ nodes Eastern Interconnection transmission test cases and 8k+ nodes taxonomy distribution test cases, are solved from arbitrary initial guesses to demonstrate the efficacy of our approach.

results.title: Mapping Disruption Sources in the Power Grid and Implications for Resilience
results.citation: Maureen S. Golan et al. (2022) Mapping Disruption Sources in the Power Grid and Implications for Resilience arXiv:2207.08146v1
results.abstract: Developing models and metrics that can address resilience against disruptions is vital to ensure power grid reliability and that adequate recovery and adaptation mechanisms are in place. In this paper, we propose a novel disruption mapping approach and apply it to the publicly available U.S. Department of Energy DOE-417 Electric Emergency and Disturbance Report to holistically analyze the origin of anomalous events and their propagation through the cyber, physical and human domains. We show that capturing the disruption process onset has implications for quantifying, mitigating, and reporting power grid resilience.

results.title: An Energy Packet Switch for Digital Power Grids
results.citation: Roberto Rojas-Cessa et al. (2018) An Energy Packet Switch for Digital Power Grids arXiv:1809.08274v1
results.abstract: We propose the design and electrical description of an energy packet switch for forwarding and delivery of energy in digital power grids in this paper. The proposed switch may receive energy from one or multiple power sources in the form of energy packets, store them and aggregate the contained energy, and forward the accumulated energy to requesting loads connected to one or multiple output ports of the switch. Energy packets are discrete amounts of energy that are associated in- or out-of-band with an address and other metadata. Loads receive these discrete amounts of finely-controlled energy rather than discretionary amounts after. The control and management of the proposed switch are based on a request-grant protocol. Using energy packets helps to manage the delivery of power in a reliable, robust, and function form that may enable features not yet available in the present power grid. The switch, as any element of a digital grid, uses a data network for the transmission of these requests and grants. The energy packet switch may be the centerpiece for creating infrastructure in the realization of the digital power grid. The design of the energy packet switch is based on shared supercapacitors to shape and manage discretization of energy. We introduce the design and analysis of the electrical properties of the proposed switch and describe the procedure used in the switch to determine the amount of energy transmitted to requesting loads.

results.title: A Hybrid Transmission Grid Architecture Enabling Efficient Optimal Power Flow
results.citation: Matthias Hotz et al. (2016) A Hybrid Transmission Grid Architecture Enabling Efficient Optimal Power Flow doi:10.1109/TPWRS.2015.2507639
results.abstract: The recent rise of electricity generation based on renewable energy sources increases the demand for transmission capacity. Capacity expansion via the upgrade of transmission line capacity, e.g., by conversion to a high-voltage direct current (HVDC) line, is an attractive option. In this paper, it is shown that if the upgrade to HVDC is applied systematically to selected transmission lines across the grid, a hybrid architecture is obtained that enables an efficient and globally optimal solution of the optimal power flow (OPF) problem. More precisely, for conventional meshed AC transmission grids the OPF problem is nonconvex and in general NP-hard, rendering it hard to solve. We prove that after the upgrade to the proposed hybrid architecture, the same mesh topology facilitates an exact convex relaxation of the OPF problem, enabling its globally optimal solution with efficient polynomial time algorithms. This OPF method is then employed in simulations, which demonstrate that the hybrid architecture can increase the effective transmission capacity and substantially reduce the generation costs, even compared to the AC grid with optimal transmission switching.

results.title: Parameterized Linear Power Flow for High Fidelity Voltage Solutions in Distribution Systems
results.citation: Marija Marković et al. (2022) Parameterized Linear Power Flow for High Fidelity Voltage Solutions in Distribution Systems doi:10.1109/TPWRS.2022.3218263
results.abstract: This paper introduces a new model for highly accurate distribution voltage solutions, coined as a parameterized linear power flow model. The proffered model is grounded on a physical model of linear power flow equations, and uses learning-aided parameterization to increase the fidelity of voltage solutions over a wide range of operating points. To this end, the closed-form analytic solution of the parameterization approach is obtained via a Gaussian Process using a deliberately small input sample and without the need for recomputation. The resulting "self-adjusting" parameter is system-specific and controls how accurate the proposed power flow equations are according to loading conditions. Under a certain value of the resulting parameter, the proposed model can fully recover the linearized formulation of a specialized branch flow model for radial distribution systems, the so-called simplified DistFlow model. Numerical examples are provided to illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed model as well as the improvement in solution accuracy for voltage magnitudes over the simplified DistFlow model and several other linear power flow models, at multiple loading levels. Simulations were carried out on six small- and medium-sized test systems.

results.title: Output Voltage Response Improvement and Ripple Reduction Control for Input-parallel Output-parallel High-Power DC Supply
results.citation: Jianhui Meng et al. (2023) Output Voltage Response Improvement and Ripple Reduction Control for Input-parallel Output-parallel High-Power DC Supply doi:10.1109/TPEL.2023.3290590
results.abstract: A three-phase isolated AC-DC-DC power supply is widely used in the industrial field due to its attractive features such as high-power density, modularity for easy expansion and electrical isolation. In high-power application scenarios, it can be realized by multiple AC-DC-DC modules with Input-Parallel Output-Parallel (IPOP) mode. However, it has the problems of slow output voltage response and large ripple in some special applications, such as electrophoresis and electroplating. This paper investigates an improved Adaptive Linear Active Disturbance Rejection Control (A-LADRC) with flexible adjustment capability of the bandwidth parameter value for the high-power DC supply to improve the output voltage response speed. To reduce the DC supply ripple, a control strategy is designed for a single module to adaptively adjust the duty cycle compensation according to the output feedback value. When multiple modules are connected in parallel, a Hierarchical Delay Current Sharing Control (HDCSC) strategy for centralized controllers is proposed to make the peaks and valleys of different modules offset each other. Finally, the proposed method is verified by designing a 42V/12000A high-power DC supply, and the results demonstrate that the proposed method is effective in improving the system output voltage response speed and reducing the voltage ripple, which has significant practical engineering application value.

results.title: Decomposition Problem in Process of Selective Identification and Localization of Voltage Fluctuations Sources in Power Grids
results.citation: Piotr Kuwałek (2022) Decomposition Problem in Process of Selective Identification and Localization of Voltage Fluctuations Sources in Power Grids doi:10.1109/ICHQP53011.2022.9808778
results.abstract: Voltage fluctuations are common disturbances in power grids, therefore the effective and selective process of identification and localization of individual voltage fluctuations sources is necessary for the minimization of such disturbances. Selectivity in the process of identification and localization disturbing loads is possible by the use cascade of blocks: demodulation, decomposition and propagation assessment. The effectiveness of this approach is closely related to the used method of decomposition. The paper presents the problem of decomposition process for the selected method of selective identification and localization of voltage fluctuation sources, in which the algorithm of enhanced empirical wavelet transform (EEWT) is used as the decomposition method. The paper presents selected research results from the real power grid, for which the result of selected approach causes mistakes in the process of identification and localization of voltage fluctuations sources. The potential causes of such mistakes related to the decomposition process are discussed on the basis of obtained research results.

results.title: Review of Power Electronic Solutions for Dielectric Barrier Discharge Applications
results.citation: Hyeongmeen Baik et al. (2025) Review of Power Electronic Solutions for Dielectric Barrier Discharge Applications doi:10.1109/TPEL.2026.3658517
results.abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive review of dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) power supply topologies, aiming to bridge the gap between DBD applications and power electronics design. Two key aspects are examined: the dependence of the DBD electrical model on reactor geometry, and application-driven requirements for injected waveform characteristics, including shapes, voltage amplitude, frequency, and modulation techniques. On this basis, the paper systematically reviews two major categories of power supplies: sinusoidal types comprising transformerless and transformer-based resonant inverters, and pulsed power supplies (PPSs). The review summarizes performance trade-offs, highlights untested topologies and emerging applications, and offers guidance for advancing high-performance DBD power supply design for next-generation systems.

results.title: Electric Vehicle Attack Impact on Power Grid Operation
results.citation: Mohammad Ali Sayed et al. (2021) Electric Vehicle Attack Impact on Power Grid Operation doi:10.1016/j.ijepes.2021.107784
results.abstract: The increasing need for reducing greenhouse gas emissions and the drive for green cities have promoted the use of electric vehicles due to their environmental benefits. In fact, countries have set their own targets and are offering incentives for people to purchase EVs as opposed to traditional gasoline-powered cars. Manufacturers have been hastily deploying charging stations to meet the charging requirements of the EVs on the road. This rapid deployment has contributed to the EV ecosystem's lack of proper security measures, raising multiple questions related to the power grid security and vulnerability. In this paper, we offer a complete examination of the EV ecosystem from the vulnerability to the attacks and finally the solutions. We start by examining the existing vulnerabilities in the EV ecosystem that can be exploited to control the EV charging and launch attacks against the power grid. We then discuss the non-linear nature of the EV charging load and simulate multiple attacks that can be launched against the power grid using these EVs. EV loads have high reactive power demand which can have a larger impact on the grid compared to residential loads. We perform simulations on two power grids and demonstrate that while the grid can recover after a 48 MW attack utilizing traditional residential loads, a smaller 30 MW EV load attack can completely destabilize the system. Finally, we suggest several patches for the existing vulnerabilities and discuss two methods aimed at detecting EV attacks.

results.title: Robust Power Flow and Three-Phase Power Flow Analyses
results.citation: Amritanshu Pandey et al. (2018) Robust Power Flow and Three-Phase Power Flow Analyses doi:10.1109/TPWRS.2018.2863042
results.abstract: Robust simulation is essential for reliable operation and planning of transmission and distribution power grids. At present, disparate methods exist for steady-state analysis of the transmission (power flow) and distribution power grid (three-phase power flow). Due to the non-linear nature of the problem, it is difficult for alternating current (AC) power flow and three-phase power flow analyses to ensure convergence to the correct physical solution, particularly from arbitrary initial conditions, or when evaluating a change (e.g. contingency) in the grid. In this paper, we describe our equivalent circuit formulation approach with current and voltage variables that models both the positive sequence network of the transmission grid and three-phase network of the distribution grid without loss of generality. The proposed circuit models and formalism enable the extension and application of circuit simulation techniques to solve for the steady-state solution with excellent robustness of convergence. Examples for positive sequence transmission and three-phase distribution systems, including actual 75k+ nodes Eastern Interconnection transmission test cases and 8k+ nodes taxonomy distribution test cases, are solved from arbitrary initial guesses to demonstrate the efficacy of our approach.

results.title: Laboratory Setup for Testing Low-Frequency Disturbances of Power Quality
results.citation: Piotr Kuwałek et al. (2024) Laboratory Setup for Testing Low-Frequency Disturbances of Power Quality doi:10.1109/CPE-POWERENG60842.2024.10604360
results.abstract: Low-frequency disturbances of power quality are one of the most common disturbances in the power grid. These disturbances are most often the result of the impact of power electronic and energy-saving devices, the number of which is increasing significantly in the power grid. Due to the simultaneous operation of various types of loads in the power grid, various types of simultaneous disturbances of power quality occur, such as voltage fluctuations and distortions. Therefore, there is a need to analyze this type of simultaneous interaction. For this purpose, a special and complementary laboratory setup has been prepared, which allows for the examination of actual states occurring in modern power networks. Selected research results are presented for this laboratory setup, which determine its basic properties. Possible applications and possibilities of the laboratory setup are presented from the point of view of current challenges.

results.title: Optimal Distributed Feedback Voltage Control under Limited Reactive Power
results.citation: Guannan Qu et al. (2018) Optimal Distributed Feedback Voltage Control under Limited Reactive Power doi:10.1109/TPWRS.2019.2931685
results.abstract: In this paper, we propose a distributed voltage control in power distribution networks through reactive power compensation. The proposed control can (i) operate in a distributed fashion where each bus makes its decision based on local voltage measurements and communication with neighboring buses, (ii) always satisfy the reactive power capacity constraint, (iii) drive the voltage magnitude into an acceptable range, and (iv) minimize an operational cost. We also perform various numerical case studies to demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the controller using the nonlinear power flow model.

papergraph_input.title: Optimal Distributed Feedback Voltage Control under Limited Reactive Power
papergraph_input.citation: Guannan Qu et al. (2018) Optimal Distributed Feedback Voltage Control under Limited Reactive Power doi:10.1109/TPWRS.2019.2931685

results.title: A New Voltage Stability-Constrained Optimal Power Flow Model: Sufficient Condition, SOCP Representation, and Relaxation
results.citation: Bai Cui et al. (2017) A New Voltage Stability-Constrained Optimal Power Flow Model: Sufficient Condition, SOCP Representation, and Relaxation doi:10.1109/TPWRS.2018.2801286
results.abstract: A simple characterization of the solvability of power flow equations is of great importance in the monitoring, control, and protection of power systems. In this paper, we introduce a sufficient condition for power flow Jacobian nonsingularity. We show that this condition is second-order conic representable when load powers are fixed. Through the incorporation of the sufficient condition, we propose a voltage stability-constrained optimal power flow (VSC-OPF) formulation as a second-order cone program (SOCP). An approximate model is introduced to improve the scalability of the formulation to larger systems. Extensive computation results on Matpower and NESTA instances confirm the effectiveness and efficiency of the formulation.

papergraph_input.citation: Bai Cui et al. (2017) A New Voltage Stability-Constrained Optimal Power Flow Model: Sufficient Condition, SOCP Representation, and Relaxation doi:10.1109/TPWRS.2018.2801286

papergraph_input.citation: Marija Marković et al. (2022) Parameterized Linear Power Flow for High Fidelity Voltage Solutions in Distribution Systems doi:10.1109/TPWRS.2022.3218263
papergraph_input.abstract: This paper introduces a new model for highly accurate distribution voltage solutions, coined as a parameterized linear power flow model. The proffered model is grounded on a physical model of linear power flow equations, and uses learning-aided parameterization to increase the fidelity of voltage solutions over a wide range of operating points. To this end, the closed-form analytic solution of the parameterization approach is obtained via a Gaussian Process using a deliberately small input sample and without the need for recomputation. The resulting "self-adjusting" parameter is system-specific and controls how accurate the proposed power flow equations are according to loading conditions. Under a certain value of the resulting parameter, the proposed model can fully recover the linearized formulation of a specialized branch flow model for radial distribution systems, the so-called simplified DistFlow model. Numerical examples are provided to illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed model as well as the improvement in solution accuracy for voltage magnitudes over the simplified DistFlow model and several other linear power flow models, at multiple loading levels. Simulations were carried out on six small- and medium-sized test systems.

results.title: Output Voltage Response Improvement and Ripple Reduction Control for Input-parallel Output-parallel High-Power DC Supply
results.citation: Jianhui Meng et al. (2023) Output Voltage Response Improvement and Ripple Reduction Control for Input-parallel Output-parallel High-Power DC Supply doi:10.1109/TPEL.2023.3290590
results.abstract: A three-phase isolated AC-DC-DC power supply is widely used in the industrial field due to its attractive features such as high-power density, modularity for easy expansion and electrical isolation. In high-power application scenarios, it can be realized by multiple AC-DC-DC modules with Input-Parallel Output-Parallel (IPOP) mode. However, it has the problems of slow output voltage response and large ripple in some special applications, such as electrophoresis and electroplating. This paper investigates an improved Adaptive Linear Active Disturbance Rejection Control (A-LADRC) with flexible adjustment capability of the bandwidth parameter value for the high-power DC supply to improve the output voltage response speed. To reduce the DC supply ripple, a control strategy is designed for a single module to adaptively adjust the duty cycle compensation according to the output feedback value. When multiple modules are connected in parallel, a Hierarchical Delay Current Sharing Control (HDCSC) strategy for centralized controllers is proposed to make the peaks and valleys of different modules offset each other. Finally, the proposed method is verified by designing a 42V/12000A high-power DC supply, and the results demonstrate that the proposed method is effective in improving the system output voltage response speed and reducing the voltage ripple, which has significant practical engineering application value.

results.title: Review of Power Electronic Solutions for Dielectric Barrier Discharge Applications
results.citation: Hyeongmeen Baik et al. (2025) Review of Power Electronic Solutions for Dielectric Barrier Discharge Applications doi:10.1109/TPEL.2026.3658517
results.abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive review of dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) power supply topologies, aiming to bridge the gap between DBD applications and power electronics design. Two key aspects are examined: the dependence of the DBD electrical model on reactor geometry, and application-driven requirements for injected waveform characteristics, including shapes, voltage amplitude, frequency, and modulation techniques. On this basis, the paper systematically reviews two major categories of power supplies: sinusoidal types comprising transformerless and transformer-based resonant inverters, and pulsed power supplies (PPSs). The review summarizes performance trade-offs, highlights untested topologies and emerging applications, and offers guidance for advancing high-performance DBD power supply design for next-generation systems.

results.title: Electric Vehicle Attack Impact on Power Grid Operation
results.citation: Mohammad Ali Sayed et al. (2021) Electric Vehicle Attack Impact on Power Grid Operation doi:10.1016/j.ijepes.2021.107784
results.abstract: The increasing need for reducing greenhouse gas emissions and the drive for green cities have promoted the use of electric vehicles due to their environmental benefits. In fact, countries have set their own targets and are offering incentives for people to purchase EVs as opposed to traditional gasoline-powered cars. Manufacturers have been hastily deploying charging stations to meet the charging requirements of the EVs on the road. This rapid deployment has contributed to the EV ecosystem's lack of proper security measures, raising multiple questions related to the power grid security and vulnerability. In this paper, we offer a complete examination of the EV ecosystem from the vulnerability to the attacks and finally the solutions. We start by examining the existing vulnerabilities in the EV ecosystem that can be exploited to control the EV charging and launch attacks against the power grid. We then discuss the non-linear nature of the EV charging load and simulate multiple attacks that can be launched against the power grid using these EVs. EV loads have high reactive power demand which can have a larger impact on the grid compared to residential loads. We perform simulations on two power grids and demonstrate that while the grid can recover after a 48 MW attack utilizing traditional residential loads, a smaller 30 MW EV load attack can completely destabilize the system. Finally, we suggest several patches for the existing vulnerabilities and discuss two methods aimed at detecting EV attacks.

results.title: Various constraints on BSM physics from extensive air showers and from ultra-high energy gamma-ray and neutrino searches
results.citation: O. Deligny (2025) Various constraints on BSM physics from extensive air showers and from ultra-high energy gamma-ray and neutrino searches arXiv:2501.19322v1
results.abstract: Various phenomena of physics beyond that of the Standard Model could occur at high scale. Ultra-high energy cosmic rays are the only particles available to explore scales above a few dozens of TeV. Although these explorations are much more limited than those carried out with colliders, they provide a series of constraints in several topics such as tests of Lorentz invariance, dark matter, phase transitions in the early universe or sterile neutrinos. Several of these constraints are reviewed in these proceedings of UHECR2024 based on searches for anomalous characteristics in extensive air showers or searches for ultra-high energy gamma rays and neutrinos.

results.title: A Hybrid Transmission Grid Architecture Enabling Efficient Optimal Power Flow
results.citation: Matthias Hotz et al. (2016) A Hybrid Transmission Grid Architecture Enabling Efficient Optimal Power Flow doi:10.1109/TPWRS.2015.2507639
results.abstract: The recent rise of electricity generation based on renewable energy sources increases the demand for transmission capacity. Capacity expansion via the upgrade of transmission line capacity, e.g., by conversion to a high-voltage direct current (HVDC) line, is an attractive option. In this paper, it is shown that if the upgrade to HVDC is applied systematically to selected transmission lines across the grid, a hybrid architecture is obtained that enables an efficient and globally optimal solution of the optimal power flow (OPF) problem. More precisely, for conventional meshed AC transmission grids the OPF problem is nonconvex and in general NP-hard, rendering it hard to solve. We prove that after the upgrade to the proposed hybrid architecture, the same mesh topology facilitates an exact convex relaxation of the OPF problem, enabling its globally optimal solution with efficient polynomial time algorithms. This OPF method is then employed in simulations, which demonstrate that the hybrid architecture can increase the effective transmission capacity and substantially reduce the generation costs, even compared to the AC grid with optimal transmission switching.

results.title: Ultra High Energy Cosmic Rays: A Short Review
results.citation: R. Aloisio (2012) Ultra High Energy Cosmic Rays: A Short Review arXiv:1211.2004v2
results.abstract: We will review the main physical aspects of Ultra High Energy Cosmic Rays. We will discuss in particular their propagation through astrophysical backgrounds, focusing on the latest experimental observations of HiRes, Telescope Array and Auger. We will also review the issue of the transition between galactic and extra-galactic cosmic rays.

results.title: Optimal Distributed Feedback Voltage Control under Limited Reactive Power
results.citation: Guannan Qu et al. (2018) Optimal Distributed Feedback Voltage Control under Limited Reactive Power doi:10.1109/TPWRS.2019.2931685
results.abstract: In this paper, we propose a distributed voltage control in power distribution networks through reactive power compensation. The proposed control can (i) operate in a distributed fashion where each bus makes its decision based on local voltage measurements and communication with neighboring buses, (ii) always satisfy the reactive power capacity constraint, (iii) drive the voltage magnitude into an acceptable range, and (iv) minimize an operational cost. We also perform various numerical case studies to demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the controller using the nonlinear power flow model.

results.title: Regression-based Inverter Control for Decentralized Optimal Power Flow and Voltage Regulation
results.citation: Oscar Sondermeijer et al. (2019) Regression-based Inverter Control for Decentralized Optimal Power Flow and Voltage Regulation arXiv:1902.08594v1
results.abstract: Electronic power inverters are capable of quickly delivering reactive power to maintain customer voltages within operating tolerances and to reduce system losses in distribution grids. This paper proposes a systematic and data-driven approach to determine reactive power inverter output as a function of local measurements in a manner that obtains near optimal results. First, we use a network model and historic load and generation data and do optimal power flow to compute globally optimal reactive power injections for all controllable inverters in the network. Subsequently, we use regression to find a function for each inverter that maps its local historical data to an approximation of its optimal reactive power injection. The resulting functions then serve as decentralized controllers in the participating inverters to predict the optimal injection based on a new local measurements. The method achieves near-optimal results when performing voltage- and capacity-constrained loss minimization and voltage flattening, and allows for an efficient volt-VAR optimization (VVO) scheme in which legacy control equipment collaborates with existing inverters to facilitate safe operation of distribution networks with higher levels of distributed generation.

results.title: Testing Methods for Ultra-High Altitude Environmental Adaptability of Power Transmission and Transformation Sensing Terminals
results.citation: Tongbo He et al. (2025) Testing Methods for Ultra-High Altitude Environmental Adaptability of Power Transmission and Transformation Sensing Terminals doi:10.1109/ICEEPS66790.2025.11239566
results.venue: 2025 4th International Conference on Energy and Electrical Power Systems (ICEEPS)
results.abstract: As core equipment for grid condition monitoring, power transmission and transformation sensing terminals face critical challenges in ultra-high altitude extreme environments, including material performance degradation, abnormal electrical characteristics, and inadequate operational adaptability. Existing environmental testing standards demonstrate limitations in simulating multi-stress coupled scenarios due to insufficient simulation capabilities and outdated control strategies. This paper proposes an ultra-high altitude adaptability testing methodology for power transmission sensing terminals based on multidimensional environmental coupling loading and dynamic gradient control. Through retrofitting a comprehensive test chamber with integrated ultraviolet irradiation, air circulation, and low-pressure control modules, we established a synchronized multi-environmental factor loading system. The implementation of a pressure-temperature-ultraviolet linkage algorithm coupled with a dynamic gradient control model enables precise simulation of altitude gradient variations. Experimental results demonstrate the method's effectiveness in replicating multidimensional extreme conditions: low pressure (≤70 kPa), intense ultraviolet radiation (≥1000 W/m2), wide temperature fluctuations (-40°C to 70°C), and strong winds (≥15 m/s), while significantly reducing testing cycle duration. This research provides technical support for reliability evaluation of power transmission equipment in ultra-high altitude regions such as the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau, demonstrating substantial engineering application value.

results.title: 500kV Ultra-High Voltage Cable Fine Manufacturing Technology Research
results.citation: Tao Zhang et al. (2025) 500kV Ultra-High Voltage Cable Fine Manufacturing Technology Research doi:10.1109/ICEMPE66159.2025.11123085
results.venue: International Conference on Electrical Materials and Power Equipment
results.abstract: Guangzhou Chuting (Suixi) power transmission and transformation project is the first 500kV ultra-high voltage urban power grid (cable) project of China Southern Power Grid, of which $500 \text{kV} 2500 ~\text{mm}^{2}$ ultra-high voltage cable is the longest transmission distance, the highest voltage level and the largest capacity of the onshore ultra-high voltage cable system in China, and it is also the power cable with the highest voltage level of international cross-linked cable. With years of experience in ultra-high voltage cable manufacturing, Qingdao Hanhe Cable Co., Ltd. has refined the connection of key core processes such as conductor stranding, insulation extrusion, wrinkled aluminum sleeve, hot melt adhesive spraying, etc., and made full use of digitalization, intelligence, informatization and other management methods to successfully complete the project product manufacturing. The completion of this project reflects the ability of localized 500kV ultra-high voltage cable, which is of great significance to promoting the high-end development of China's cable project.

results.title: Modeling and Enhancement of Seismic Resilience for Ultra High Voltage Converter Station
results.citation: Zhihang Xue et al. (2024) Modeling and Enhancement of Seismic Resilience for Ultra High Voltage Converter Station doi:10.1109/ICPEE64457.2024.10949981
results.venue: 2024 8th International Conference on Power and Energy Engineering (ICPEE)
results.abstract: The power system is a critical infrastructure for the development of the national economy, and earthquakes pose a significant threat to the operational safety of the power grid. As a key node for long-distance electrical power transmission, the ability of ultra-high voltage (UHV) converter stations to withstand earthquake and recover swiftly afterward has garnered considerable attention within the industry. This article focuses on the modeling and enhancement methods for the seismic resistance of UHV converter station. Firstly, a component diagram of the UHV converter stations is established, and the post-earthquake transmission function index for individual station is defined. Subsequently, based on seismic intensity data, the failure probability of equipment within each branch at a specified seismic intensity is calculated. Additionally, the seismic resilience index R of the UHV converter station is defined, considering the post-earthquake recovery process. Finally, using data from a specific ±800 kV UHV converter station as a case study, its seismic resilience is assessed. Through comparative analysis, methods to enhance seismic resilience are explored. The research holds significant guidance for enhancing the seismic resilience of the UHV voltage converter stations.

results.title: Consensus-based feature selection and Bayesian-optimized stacking ensembles for comprehensive fault diagnosis in series-compensated ultra-high-voltage power systems
results.citation: Rab Nawaz et al. (2026) Consensus-based feature selection and Bayesian-optimized stacking ensembles for comprehensive fault diagnosis in series-compensated ultra-high-voltage power systems doi:10.1038/s41598-026-49195-7
results.venue: Scientific Reports
results.abstract: Modern power systems incorporate ultra-high-voltage transmission lines with series compensation to enhance their reliability and stability. These configurations generate complex data patterns in electrical signals, which present challenges for accurate fault diagnosis. This complexity arises from various potential faults, such as high-impedance faults and inductive-capacitive interactions, compounded by data scarcity owing to privacy and cybersecurity constraints. This study employs a simulation-based analysis to facilitate fault detection, classification, and localization as unified Multi-Classification tasks. The methodology improves the time-frequency domain data through meta-feature engineering and utilizes consensus-based feature selection by combining gradient boosting, variance thresholding, and mutual information. Model learning is conducted using Bayesian optimization within a Stacking Ensemble, integrating Extra Trees, Light Gradient Boosting Machine, and Extreme Gradient Boosting with an optimized Random Forest meta-classifier. The evaluation considers the accuracy, computational time, inference speed, and memory usage under challenging conditions to address practical limitations. The results indicate that the Stacking Ensemble achieves the highest performance despite its greater computational demand. Extra Trees offers the fastest execution but shows decreased accuracy with increased classification complexity. The Light Gradient Boosting Machine and Extreme Gradient Boosting demonstrate optimized performance across tasks owing to enhanced data processing. These findings confirm the practical applicability of the proposed fault diagnosis framework to modern ultra-high-voltage power systems.

results.title: Technical Transformation Scheme and Practice Applied in Ultra-high Share of Renewable Energy Power System at County-level Scale
results.citation: Ping Xiong et al. (2025) Technical Transformation Scheme and Practice Applied in Ultra-high Share of Renewable Energy Power System at County-level Scale doi:10.1109/ICPST65050.2025.11088518
results.venue: 2025 IEEE 3rd International Conference on Power Science and Technology (ICPST)
results.abstract: The rapid integration of variable renewable energy into county-level power grids has introduced significant operational challenges, particularly transmission congestion and transformer overload caused by bi-directional flow. To overcome the restriction of conventional grid reinforcement solutions focused mainly on infrastructure expansion, the work develops an integrated generation-grid-load-storage coordination framework adapted to scenarios with high penetration of renewables. The solution achieves localized energy balance through optimized energy storage allocation and operational modes control, establishing a resilient power grid architecture capable of grid-connected interaction and autonomous islanded operation. Renovation schemes of building a power system with ultra-high renewable penetration ratios are presented, in which technical routes for the coordination framework are investigated. Performance of key technologies, including grid-forming voltage source converters, smooth mode transition, and the collective control of the coordination system in support of the independent operation and stability of a 100% variable inverter-based renewable energy sources power system, is validated.

papergraph_input.venue: 2025 IEEE 3rd International Conference on Power Science and Technology (ICPST)
papergraph_input.abstract: The rapid integration of variable renewable energy into county-level power grids has introduced significant operational challenges, particularly transmission congestion and transformer overload caused by bi-directional flow. To overcome the restriction of conventional grid reinforcement solutions focused mainly on infrastructure expansion, the work develops an integrated generation-grid-load-storage coordination framework adapted to scenarios with high penetration of renewables. The solution achieves localized energy balance through optimized energy storage allocation and operational modes control, establishing a resilient power grid architecture capable of grid-connected interaction and autonomous islanded operation. Renovation schemes of building a power system with ultra-high renewable penetration ratios are presented, in which technical routes for the coordination framework are investigated. Performance of key technologies, including grid-forming voltage source converters, smooth mode transition, and the collective control of the coordination system in support of the independent operation and stability of a 100% variable inverter-based renewable energy sources power system, is validated.

results.title: Exploration of Ultra-High-Voltage Alternating Current Power Transmission Technology Standardization for Global Energy Interconnection
results.citation: Zhou Zhou et al. (2021) Exploration of Ultra-High-Voltage Alternating Current Power Transmission Technology Standardization for Global Energy Interconnection doi:10.1109/ACPEE51499.2021.9437070
results.venue: 2021 6th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
results.abstract: The development of Global Energy Interconnection (GEI), which projects to build a globally interconnected power grid to dispatch electricity generated by renewable energy worldwide, has become a strong driving force to the electrical engineering industry. Ultra-High-Voltage (GEI), consists Alternating Current (AC) and Direct Current (DC) technologies establish the backbone of GEI and it plays an important role in GEI, and it significantly promotes the upgrade of high voltage, electronics, materials, information and other related technologies. With its fast development, there is a urgent demand for the standardization of the technologies. In this paper, we would focus on the UHV AC standardization exploration: First, we present a brief introduction of the background. Second, we demonstrate current development situation of UHV AC standards. Third, we explore the requirement of of UHV AC standards with the development of GEI. Forth, we propose the standard system from the four major areas of general and design, equipment and test, construction, and operation. Fifth, an efficient roadmap is given.

results.title: Particle Swarm Optimization with Stagnation Counter Technique for Optimization of Main Installation Materials Cost in Power Transmission and Transformation Projects
results.citation: Yuliang Jiang et al. (2026) Particle Swarm Optimization with Stagnation Counter Technique for Optimization of Main Installation Materials Cost in Power Transmission and Transformation Projects doi:10.1109/ICICACS68679.2026.11578464
results.venue: 2026 4th International Conference on Integrated Circuits and Communication Systems (ICICACS)
results.abstract: The cost-effective power transmission is the bulk movement of electrical energy form generating stations to substations located near demand centers. The long distance using high voltage lines and it serves as the critical bridge in the electrical grid, ensuring that energy produced as efficient, remote location reaches homes and industries. The conductors, insulators, towers and transformers contribute major portion of the total investment in power transmission and transformation projects. Selection of suitable main installation materials configurations is crucial for minimizing project costs while maintaining high transmission efficiency and system reliability. The existing optimization algorithms focus on overall project investment and faced the inefficient energy transmission problem, when the existing algorithm are unable to concentrate the multiple crucial aspects simultaneously and stuck into the local optima. To mitigate this problem, the Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm with Stagnation Counter Technique (SCT) is used proposed. The PSO is used to concentrate the multiple aspects simultaneously based on the iterative search spaces utilizing swarm collective experience recursively. The SCT is used to overcome the premature convergence problem which is occurred by the PSO, by forcing the algorithm to resume the exploration when the progress is stopped. The proposed PSO-SCT framework is achieved better results comparing to some models such as Standard Particle Swarm Optimization (SPSO), Fully Informed Particle Swarm Optimization (FIPSO). Thus, the proposed PSO-SCT based approach optimizes conductor type, conductor cross-sectional area, transformer capacity, tower configuration, and insulation selection to reduce main installation materials cost while satisfying transmission-efficiency and reliability constraints.

results.title: A Simulation Study on Grounding Faults in the Metal Shield Layer of High-Voltage Power Cables
results.citation: Zifan Zhang et al. (2025) A Simulation Study on Grounding Faults in the Metal Shield Layer of High-Voltage Power Cables doi:10.1109/ICLCSE66889.2025.11389670
results.venue: 2025 International Conference on Low Carbon and Smart Energy (ICLCSE)
results.abstract: In the context of accelerating urbanisation and the transformation of energy structures, high voltage power cables have emerged as a pivotal transmission method within contemporary power systems. The operational reliability of these entities has been demonstrated to have a direct impact on grid security and power supply quality. Within cable structures, the metallic shielding layer fulfils a dual function: it both suppresses electromagnetic interference and protects communication facilities, while also providing a safe return path for ground faults and preventing electric shock accidents. The present study investigates the mechanisms and characteristics of short-circuit faults in cable shields. Utilising the electromagnetic transient simulation software PSCAD, a refined cable model incorporating frequency-dependent parameters was constructed. The electrical characteristics and influencing factors of such faults were revealed through analysis of the variation patterns of shield currents and zero-sequence currents under different operating conditions. The present research provides both theoretical and empirical support for the rapid diagnosis and condition assessment in engineering applications.

results.title: Study on Ecological Environmental Impact Assessment Index System of UHV AC Transmission and Transformation Engineering-A Case Study of 1000kv UHV Transmission and Transformation Engineering from Zhangbei to Xiongan
results.citation: Jiming Song et al. (2023) Study on Ecological Environmental Impact Assessment Index System of UHV AC Transmission and Transformation Engineering-A Case Study of 1000kv UHV Transmission and Transformation Engineering from Zhangbei to Xiongan doi:10.1051/e3sconf/202337002001
results.venue: E3S Web of Conferences
results.abstract: Ultra-high voltage (UHV) power grid is a new transmission and transformation technology, which has the advantages of large transmission capacity, long transmission distance, low line loss, saving land resources and obvious economic benefits. Taking the Zhangbei-xiongan 1000kv AC high voltage power transmission and transformation project as an example, this paper constructs the eco-environmental index system of the UHV transmission and transformation project by using the pressure-state-response model, and constructs a set of 18 indexes eco-environmental index system, it mainly includes three aspects: the eco-environmental pressure index caused by the construction behavior in different construction stages of power transmission and transformation projects, and the eco-environmental state index given by on-the-spot investigation and study of projects, based on the construction unit on the ecological environment impact management response measures given indicators. The index system provides a basis for comprehensive and systematic evaluation of the impact of power transmission and distribution projects on the ecological environment, and finally draws the corresponding project construction evaluation conclusions. In this paper, the eco-environmental impact assessment index system of power transmission and transformation engineering has certain universality, and it has certain reference and guiding significance for the ecological environment evaluation of power transmission and transformation engineering.

results.title: The Seismic Response of Ultra-High Voltage Gas-Insulated Transmission Lines
results.citation: Wei Lv et al. (2023) The Seismic Response of Ultra-High Voltage Gas-Insulated Transmission Lines doi:10.1088/1742-6596/2557/1/012065
results.venue: Journal of Physics: Conference Series
results.abstract: As an important part of the substation, the seismic capacity of ultra-high voltage (UHV) electrical equipment is related to the safe and stable operation of the substation and the whole power grid. The UHV gas-insulated transmission lines (GIL) have many structural characteristics, such as long pipeline distance, the large span between supports and the obvious amplification effect of supports. Its natural frequency is close to the predominant frequency of the seismic wave, resulting in its susceptibility to resonance or resonance-like and high seismic susceptibility. In order to analyze the seismic performance of UHV GIL better, the vibration mode decomposition response spectrum method and the dynamic time-history analysis method are used to analyze the seismic response of UHV GIL in this paper. The search for seismic weak positions of GIL is a reference for effectively improving the seismic performance of UHV GIL.

results.title: Development of High Voltage Direct Current (HVDC) Transmission Systems for Efficient Power Transfer
results.citation: Naveen Garg et al. (2025) Development of High Voltage Direct Current (HVDC) Transmission Systems for Efficient Power Transfer doi:10.1109/CISES66934.2025.11264962
results.venue: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent and Secure Engineering Solutions (CISES)
results.abstract: The direct transmission systems using the voltage of one of the largest values, HVDC, that allow transmitting electricity with high efficiency and safety over long distances may be considered one of the most important milestones in the history of electricity engineering and development of the electrical power systems. It has a few strengths such as; it has a lower power loss compared with standard alternating current (AC) systems, is more stable on the grid, and has power density. The fundamental units of the HVDC technology are transformer stations, lines and control systems. HVDC converters technology has made the major focus on voltage source converters (VSCs) and line-commutated converters (LCCs) in power conversion between AC and DC to achieve maximum efficiency of the system. Renewable energy: Here HVDC can adopt renewable energy sources like wind power and solar energy to ease transfer of power through modular and long-distance power transmission of remote generation sites to the centre of consumption. Technology HVDC is being developed in the direction of higher converter efficiency, reduced cost and decreased fault tolerance. Enhanced control algorithms and real-time monitoring software guarantee dynamic stability and easy interface with current grid infrastructures. With the information on the cleaner energy future, an even further progress of the HVDC systems that will be incorporated in the world energy transition on the way to more sustainable and resilient electricity power systems is proceeding.

results.title: Voltage Control Partitioning Method Based on Weighted Fast-Newman for Ultra High Voltage Direct Current Power Grid
results.citation: Tianhua Chen et al. (2024) Voltage Control Partitioning Method Based on Weighted Fast-Newman for Ultra High Voltage Direct Current Power Grid doi:10.1109/IFEEA64237.2024.10878526
results.venue: 2024 11th International Forum on Electrical Engineering and Automation (IFEEA)
results.abstract: With the infeed UHV-DC merges into the power grid, the division of voltage control partitioning is more challenging, which is an important step in the secondary voltage control of power system. In this paper, the infeed UHV-DC node is equivalent to a reactive power supply, the weighted Fast-Newman algorithm is used to merge the reactive power sources through the maximum distance difference degree. The proposed method makes load nodes be divided with voltage reactive sensitivity and the average power factor. Which not only reflects the power grid by the center of hub AC bus control requirements, and also conforms to the principle of power grid control hierarchical control. By using the method the control partition result is more adaptable and the influence of UHV-DC transmission power of the partition is considered as well. The rationality of the proposed method is verified by the simulation results of IEEE39 node system.

results.title: Testing Methods for Ultra-High Altitude Environmental Adaptability of Power Transmission and Transformation Sensing Terminals
results.citation: Tongbo He et al. (2025) Testing Methods for Ultra-High Altitude Environmental Adaptability of Power Transmission and Transformation Sensing Terminals doi:10.1109/ICEEPS66790.2025.11239566
results.venue: 2025 4th International Conference on Energy and Electrical Power Systems (ICEEPS)
results.abstract: As core equipment for grid condition monitoring, power transmission and transformation sensing terminals face critical challenges in ultra-high altitude extreme environments, including material performance degradation, abnormal electrical characteristics, and inadequate operational adaptability. Existing environmental testing standards demonstrate limitations in simulating multi-stress coupled scenarios due to insufficient simulation capabilities and outdated control strategies. This paper proposes an ultra-high altitude adaptability testing methodology for power transmission sensing terminals based on multidimensional environmental coupling loading and dynamic gradient control. Through retrofitting a comprehensive test chamber with integrated ultraviolet irradiation, air circulation, and low-pressure control modules, we established a synchronized multi-environmental factor loading system. The implementation of a pressure-temperature-ultraviolet linkage algorithm coupled with a dynamic gradient control model enables precise simulation of altitude gradient variations. Experimental results demonstrate the method's effectiveness in replicating multidimensional extreme conditions: low pressure (≤70 kPa), intense ultraviolet radiation (≥1000 W/m2), wide temperature fluctuations (-40°C to 70°C), and strong winds (≥15 m/s), while significantly reducing testing cycle duration. This research provides technical support for reliability evaluation of power transmission equipment in ultra-high altitude regions such as the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau, demonstrating substantial engineering application value.

results.title: An active de-duplication backup algorithm for storage data in ultra-high-voltage power network based on improved swarm algorithm
results.citation: Ying Zhang (2024) An active de-duplication backup algorithm for storage data in ultra-high-voltage power network based on improved swarm algorithm doi:10.1117/12.3034240
results.venue: Symposium on Advances in Electrical, Electronics and Computer Engineering
results.abstract: The source network load and storage data of Ultra High Voltage (UHV) power grid is very large, including massive real-time monitoring data and equipment status data. The backup of these data needs to occupy a lot of storage space, and the transmission and backup process may put forward high requirements on the network bandwidth and transmission speed. Therefore, an active de-duplication backup algorithm based on improved swarm algorithm is proposed for UHV power grid source data storage. The bee colony algorithm is improved and optimized as artificial bee colony algorithm. Simhash algorithm is introduced to remove load and storage data of UHV power grid. Based on this, the balanced binary tree is used to realize its backup. The experimental results show that: Compared with the traditional method, when using the research method to backup data, the storage space is minimum, the data deduplication rate is higher than 95%, and the time is always less than 100ms.

results.title: Analysis of the Impact of Transmitter-Side Electrochemical Energy Storage on Recipient-Side Phase Change Failure in Ultra-High Voltage Direct Current Transmission Systems
results.citation: Yiheng Ren (2024) Analysis of the Impact of Transmitter-Side Electrochemical Energy Storage on Recipient-Side Phase Change Failure in Ultra-High Voltage Direct Current Transmission Systems doi:10.1109/EEBDA60612.2024.10485721
results.venue: 2024 IEEE 3rd International Conference on Electrical Engineering, Big Data and Algorithms (EEBDA)
results.abstract: Phase change failure is one of the common faults in UHV DC transmission system, which leads to voltage fluctuation, current inrush and other phenomena, thus causing serious harm to the safe operation of power grid. Studying the influencing factors of phase change failure is a prerequisite for suppressing phase change failure, and electrochemical energy storage at the sending end can suppress voltage fluctuation, mitigate current impact, improve system inertia, and play a certain buffer and auxiliary role in the case of phase change failure at the receiving end. Therefore, this paper establishes a set of UHV DC transmission system model and explores the influencing factors of phase change failure. Based on this model, the influence mechanism of electrochemical energy storage at the sending end on the phase change failure at the receiving end is investigated from the three aspects of output power size, inductance size and energy storage capacity. The results show that: (1) the larger the output force is, the lesser the degree of phase change failure is. (2) The larger the grounding inductance is, the lower the degree of phase change failure is. (3) The larger the energy storage capacity is, the lower the degree of phase change failure is.
